function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{

    }
  ]
}

I have a lot of useful information. Let me now think about the question:

The question asks: If GPU procurement costs declined 50%, what would be the implied effect on AI FCF proxy at Q1 2026 run-rates assuming all else equal? Which filing disclosures limit confidence in such an estimate?

Key data points I have:
1. AI CapEx Q1 2026: $7,723M
2. AI FCF proxy Q1 2026: ~$(8,321)M (calculated as EBITDA-based operating cash flow proxy minus CapEx)
3. GPU/processor procurement is approximately 70% of total capital cost of building compute infrastructure (per SemiAnalysis)
4. The filing mentions GPU depreciation expense increase of $908M in Q1 2026 R&D
5. AI segment D&A: $1,493M Q1 2026
6. AI segment operating loss: $(2,469)M Q1 2026
7. AI segment revenue: $818M Q1 2026
8. AI segment share-based compensation: $378M Q1 2026

Now, the question is about GPU procurement costs declining 50%. Let me think about this carefully.

GPU procurement costs are part of CapEx. The filing says ~70% of total capital cost of building compute infrastructure comes from procuring processors and critical IT equipment (GPUs).

So if GPU procurement costs declined 50%, the implied effect on AI FCF proxy would be:
- AI CapEx Q1 2026: $7,723M
- GPU portion of CapEx: ~70% × $7,723M = $5,406M (assuming all AI CapEx is compute infrastructure)
- 50% decline in GPU procurement costs: $5,406M × 50% = $2,703M reduction in CapEx
- New AI FCF proxy: $(8,321)M + $2,703M = $(5,618)M

But wait, I need to be careful here. The 70% figure is for "AI companies without a build cost advantage" - SpaceX/xAI may have a build cost advantage. Also, the 70% is for "total capital cost of building compute infrastructure" - not all AI CapEx is necessarily compute infrastructure.

## Implied Effect of a 50% Decline in GPU Procurement Costs on AI FCF Proxy at Q1 2026 Run-Rates

### Step 1: Construct the Q1 2026 AI FCF Proxy

The S-1 does not disclose a segment-level free cash flow figure, so an analytical proxy must be constructed from disclosed segment data. Using the standard EBITDA-based approximation (Segment Adjusted EBITDA − CapEx), where Segment Adjusted EBITDA is defined in the filing as segment income (loss) from operations excluding D&A, share-based compensation, restructuring charges, and impairment:

| Component (Q1 2026, $M) | Amount |
|---|---|
| AI Revenue | $818 |
| AI Loss from operations | $(2,469) |
| Add back: D&A | $1,493 |
| Add back: Share-based compensation | $378 |
| Restructuring credit | $(11) |
| **AI Segment Adjusted EBITDA (proxy for operating cash flow)** | **$(598)** |
| Less: AI Capital expenditures | $(7,723) |
| **AI FCF Proxy (Q1 2026)** | **$(8,321)** |

Annualized Q1 2026 run-rate AI FCF proxy ≈ $(8,321) × 4 = **$(33,284) million**.

### Step 2: Estimate the GPU Procurement Component of AI CapEx

The S-1 cites SemiAnalysis data stating that for "most AI companies without a build cost advantage," approximately **70%** of the total capital cost of building compute infrastructure derives from "procuring processors and critical IT equipment" (i.e., GPUs and related hardware), with ~30% from data center construction.

Applying this industry benchmark to Q1 2026 AI CapEx:
- GPU/processor procurement portion ≈ 70% × $7,723M = **$5,406M** (quarterly)
- Annualized run-rate ≈ $5,406M × 4 = **$21,624M**

### Step 3: Apply the 50% Decline (All Else Equal)

A 50% decline in GPU procurement costs reduces CapEx by:
- Quarterly reduction: 50% × $5,406M = **$2,703M**
- Annualized run-rate reduction: 50% × $21,624M = **$10,812M**

### Step 4: Implied New AI FCF Proxy

| Metric | Baseline (Q1 2026) | After 50% GPU Cost Decline | Improvement |
|---|---|---|---|
| AI FCF Proxy (quarterly) | $(8,321)M | **$(5,618)M** | +$2,703M |
| AI FCF Proxy (annualized run-rate) | $(33,284)M | **$(22,472)M** | +$10,812M |
| % Improvement | — | — | **~32.5%** |

**Bottom line:** Under the mechanical "all else equal" assumption and using the SemiAnalysis 70% industry benchmark, a 50% decline in GPU procurement costs would improve the Q1 2026 annualized AI FCF proxy by approximately **$10.8 billion**, narrowing the run-rate cash burn from ~$(33.3)B to ~$(22.5)B — roughly a one-third reduction in the AI segment's cash deficit.

---

## Filing Disclosures That Limit Confidence in This Estimate

The mechanical calculation above rests on several assumptions that the S-1 either does not support or actively undercuts. The following disclosures materially limit confidence:

### 1. The 70% GPU cost split is an industry benchmark, not the company's actual cost structure
The filing explicitly frames the SemiAnalysis 70%/30% split as applying to "most AI companies **without a build cost advantage**." SpaceX/xAI claims it **does** have a build cost advantage, stating it "achieved data center construction costs for COLOSSUS II that are considerably lower than industry benchmarks on a per megawatt basis" and touts a "dual speed and cost advantage." If the company's data center construction costs are lower than the industry 30% benchmark, then GPU/processor procurement could represent **more than 70%** of its compute CapEx — but the filing never quantifies its own split. The 70% figure is therefore an unverified proxy.

### 2. "Processors and critical IT equipment" is broader than GPUs alone
The 70% bucket includes networking equipment, memory, storage, and other critical IT hardware — not just GPUs. The filing does not isolate GPU procurement costs as a subset, so applying the full 70% to "GPU procurement costs" overstates the GPU-specific component.

### 3. No quantitative breakdown of AI CapEx is disclosed
The S-1 provides only aggregate AI segment CapEx ($7,723M in Q1 2026) and qualitative language referencing "obtaining third-party GPUs, manufacturing our own GPUs, and constructing, leasing, maintaining, enhancing, and expanding our data centers." There is no line-item decomposition into GPU procurement vs. data center construction vs. other components.

### 4. The company manufactures its own GPUs
The filing states CapEx includes costs related to "manufacturing our own GPUs" (referencing the Terafab initiative with Tesla/Intel). A decline in third-party GPU procurement prices would not necessarily reduce internal GPU manufacturing costs, which are driven by different inputs (silicon wafers, fab capacity, labor). The filing does not disclose the mix between third-party procurement and internal manufacturing.

### 5. The AI FCF proxy is an analytical construct, not a disclosed metric
The S-1 defines **Segment Adjusted EBITDA** but does **not** disclose segment-level operating cash flow or free cash flow. Cash flow statements are presented only on a consolidated basis (Q1 2026 consolidated operating cash flow was $1,047M; investing outflow was $(16,724)M). The "AI FCF proxy" used here (Segment Adjusted EBITDA − segment CapEx) excludes working capital changes, taxes, and interest — all of which could be material for a rapidly scaling, debt-financed AI buildout.

### 6. The "all else equal" assumption ignores the lagged depreciation benefit
GPU depreciation is a major operating cost driver: the filing attributes a **$908M year-over-year increase in R&D expense in Q1 2026** (and $1,673M in FY 2025) to GPU depreciation, with servers/networking equipment depreciated over **5–6 years**. A 50% decline in GPU procurement costs would eventually reduce depreciation on newly purchased GPUs, improving Segment Adjusted EBITDA — but only with a multi-year lag and only on incremental purchases. The "all else equal" calculation captures only the immediate CapEx reduction and omits this second-order operating-cost benefit, meaning the estimate is conservative on the operating side but also incomplete.

### 7. Cloud computing expenses are a separate, unaffected cost
The filing cites **$301M** of higher cloud computing and data center infrastructure expenses in Q1 2026 R&D (and $1,440M in FY 2025). These third-party cloud capacity costs would not decline with GPU procurement prices, yet they are embedded in the AI operating loss and thus in the FCF proxy denominator.

### 8. Non-cancelable contractual commitments and related-party leases are locked in
As of March 31, 2026, the company has **$25,451M** in non-cancelable contractual commitments "primarily relat[ing] to the Company's investments in AI infrastructure and third-party cloud capacity arrangements." Additionally, the **Valor** related-party equipment leases (failed sale-leasebacks) carry debt of ~$1,121M current + $7,920M non-current as of March 31, 2026, with **Valor Transaction III** adding another **$6,587M** in April 2026. These obligations are contractually fixed and would not benefit from a decline in GPU procurement costs, meaning a meaningful portion of near-term AI infrastructure spending is insulated from the hypothetical price decline.

### 9. Post-quarter transactions suggest the Q1 2026 run-rate understates forward CapEx
The filing discloses several material AI infrastructure transactions **after** March 31, 2026: the Valor III lease ($6,587M), turbine acquisitions (~$2,925M combined), and the Anthropic cloud services agreement (monthly fees through May 2029). These suggest the Q1 2026 annualized run-rate of ~$30.9B AI CapEx may understate the actual forward trajectory, complicating any "run-rate" extrapolation.

### 10. No GPU pricing, volume, or vendor disclosure
The S-1 references NVIDIA H100 power consumption (1.3 kW/GPU) and a market rental rate ($3.33/hour per Silicon Data) only as industry context. It does **not** disclose the company's actual GPU unit costs, procurement volumes, vendor mix, or hedging arrangements, making it impossible to verify what "GPU procurement costs" even represent in dollar terms for the company.

---

### Conclusion

Using the SemiAnalysis 70% industry benchmark mechanically, a 50% decline in GPU procurement costs would improve the Q1 2026 annualized AI FCF proxy by approximately **$10.8 billion** (from ~$(33.3)B to ~$(22.5)B), a ~32.5% reduction in cash burn. However, confidence in this estimate is materially limited because: (i) the 70% split is an industry benchmark that the company claims it exceeds via its build cost advantage, yet does not quantify; (ii) the filing provides no GPU-specific CapEx breakdown; (iii) the company manufactures some of its own GPUs, whose costs would not track third-party procurement prices; (iv) the AI FCF proxy is an analytical construct, not a disclosed metric, and excludes working capital, taxes, and interest; (v) substantial non-cancelable commitments and related-party lease debt are contractually fixed; and (vi) the "all else equal" assumption omits the lagged depreciation benefit that would further improve operating cash flow over time. The estimate should therefore be treated as an illustrative upper-bound sensitivity rather than a precise forecast.
